David's crisis arrives without warning. He returns from serving the Philistines only to find Ziklag burned, his wives captured, and his men ready to stone him. This is the moment that breaks men. His families are gone. His reputation is ashes. His army is turning against him. Yet in verse 6, we encounter something that cuts against the entire texture of human desperation: "David found strength in the LORD his God." This is not optimism. This is not denial. David weeps like everyone else. But he turns toward God rather than away, and that singular act becomes the hinge on which everything turns. He asks God directly: Should I pursue? Will I succeed? God answers with absolute clarity. Pursue. You will overtake. You will recover. In this moment, David teaches us something ruthlessly real about faith—it is not the absence of fear or grief, but the deliberate choice to believe God's word when everything around you screams that belief is foolish.

What follows is a masterclass in obedience under pressure. David pursues with four hundred men while two hundred collapse in exhaustion. They discover an abandoned Egyptian slave—a foreigner, a stranger, someone with every reason to be invisible. David's men feed him. This kindness to a stranger seems almost incidental in the narrative, but nothing in Scripture is accidental. This forsaken man becomes their guide to the Amalekites' camp. They strike from twilight through the next evening. Everything is recovered. Everyone comes home. But notice what David does not do: He does not celebrate himself. He does not declare himself the mighty warrior. He does not hoard the plunder. When his own men—"wicked and worthless men" as Scripture names them—refuse to share the recovery with those too exhausted to fight, David intervenes with a principle that will outlive him: "The share of the one who went to battle will match the share of the one who stayed with the supplies. They will share alike." He then distributes the spoils to the elders throughout Judah, calling it "plunder of the LORD's enemies," not David's trophy.

This is the character of a man being prepared for a throne. David understands something fundamental that his men had forgotten: victory belongs to God, and therefore generosity becomes a form of worship. His justice toward the exhausted soldiers is not sentimental—it is theological. If the LORD gave the victory, then the LORD determines who shares in it, and strength and stamina are not the measure of a man's worth in God's eyes. The plunder distributed to the elders is not political calculation but recognition that David's survival has been bound up with the loyalty and kindness of Judah's people. Everything in David's response—his seeking God's word, his kindness to the foreigner, his refusal to let his army devour itself, his recognition that all good things belong to God—shapes him into someone fit to shepherd a nation. And it all flows from that moment when a broken man, facing ruin, chose to find strength in the LORD. The question Scripture leaves with us is immediate: When your world burns, where do you turn?
